title: The Khazars
summary: The Khazars were a Turkic people of the western Eurasian steppe who built a wealthy, multiethnic trading khaganate north of the Caucasus, between the Black and Caspian seas, from roughly the seventh to the tenth century. Sitting at a crossroads of the Byzantine, Islamic, and steppe worlds, they are best remembered because their ruling house and at least part of the elite adopted Judaism, probably in stages, and because they held back Arab expansion into eastern Europe, before the Rus' destroyed their capital around 965.
hook: Sometime in the eighth or ninth century, the king of a Turkic steppe empire did something no other medieval ruler did. Pressed on the west by Christian Byzantium and on the south by the Muslim caliphate, both eager to claim him, he adopted neither. Instead the Khazar court became Jewish, taking up the faith of a people who held no state and no army anywhere on earth. How far the conversion spread, and exactly when, is argued to this day. That it happened at all remains one of the strangest facts in medieval history.

beginner.thePeriod:
Around the year 900, a traveler heading north from Baghdad would cross a wide grassland and reach a busy river city on the lower Volga. The city was called Atil. It was the capital of the Khazars. In its markets, people spoke many languages. Traders swapped furs, honey, wax, and captured slaves from the northern forests for silver coins, silk, and spices from the Muslim south. The Khazars taxed all of this trade. That is how they grew rich and strong.

The Khazars were a Turkic people from the grassland steppe. For about three centuries, roughly the seventh to the tenth, they ruled a large empire north of the Caucasus Mountains. Their land sat between the Black Sea and the Caspian Sea. This was a crossroads. To the west stood the Christian empire of Byzantium. To the south lay the Muslim caliphate. The Khazars sat between these two giants and held their own.
beginner.theBoundaries:
The Khazar state grew out of an older steppe empire. In the 600s, a large Turkic confederation to the east broke apart. The Khazars rose from its western half. They built their own khaganate, led by a ruler called the khagan.

Their first great test came from the south. Arab armies had conquered Persia and were pushing north through the mountain passes. For more than a century, the Khazars fought them. The wars were long and hard. Cities changed hands. Both sides raided deep into enemy land. In the end, the Khazars held the mountain line. The Arab advance into eastern Europe stopped there. Many historians think this mattered a great deal. Without the Khazar wall, Arab power might have pushed much farther north.

The end came from a new direction. In the north, a people called the Rus' were growing strong. The Rus' were warrior-traders of Scandinavian origin who ruled over Slavic lands. Around the year 965, their prince Sviatoslav attacked the Khazars. He smashed their fortress at Sarkel. A few years later he destroyed Atil itself. The Khazar empire never recovered.
beginner.theConditions:
The Khazars did not build their power on farming alone. They built it on movement. Their lands controlled the great rivers and roads that linked the northern forests to the southern markets. Whoever held these routes could tax the goods that flowed along them. The Khazars held them for a long time.

Their capital, Atil, sat where the Volga River meets the Caspian Sea. It was really two towns on opposite banks, joined by boats. People of many faiths lived there. Muslims, Christians, Jews, and followers of older steppe religions all had a place. Arab writers describe a court with separate judges for each religious community. A visitor could find a mosque, a church, and a synagogue in the same city. This mix was not an accident. A trading empire needed traders of every kind, and it needed them to feel safe.
beginner.internalDiversity:
Power at the top was split in an unusual way. The Khazars had two rulers. One was the khagan, a sacred figure who was honored but rarely seen. The other was the bek, a war-leader who ran the army and the government. The khagan gave the state its holiness. The bek made the real decisions. This strange double crown puzzled foreign visitors.

The most famous thing about the Khazars is their religion. At some point, the ruling family and part of the nobility became Jews. This probably happened in the 700s or 800s. It set them apart, since their powerful neighbors were Christian and Muslim. By choosing Judaism, the Khazar kings avoided taking sides between the two empires that pressed on them. A later Jewish letter, said to come from a Khazar king named Joseph, tells the story of the choice. It says an early ruler named Bulan compared the three faiths and picked the faith of Israel. We cannot be sure how much of this tale is true. We also do not know how many ordinary Khazars followed their kings into Judaism. Most probably did not. But that the royal court kept the Jewish faith is well supported.
beginner.longConsequences:
When Atil fell, the Khazar empire vanished from the map. No Khazar kingdom rose again. Over time the people scattered and mixed into other groups. Their language died out. Today no one speaks Khazar, and no book written by the Khazars themselves survives in full. Almost everything we know comes from their neighbors' writings and from things dug out of the ground.

Still, the Khazars left marks. By stopping the Arab armies, they helped shape which parts of eastern Europe stayed outside the Muslim world. By taxing the river trade, they helped feed the silver that flowed into the north. And their fall opened the road for the Rus', whose state grew into the beginnings of Russia and Ukraine. The Khazars are gone, but the map they helped draw is not.

There is also a modern myth. Some people claim that today's Ashkenazi Jews are descended from the Khazars, not from ancient Israel. This idea turns up in certain corners, but scholars and geneticists have found little to support it. It is best treated as a legend, not a fact.
beginner.periodizationDebate:
Historians usually say the Khazar khaganate lasted from about the mid-600s to the year 965. But both ends are fuzzy. The Khazars broke away from the older Turkic world slowly, over many years. It is hard to name the exact moment they became their own state. The ending is unclear too. Sviatoslav wrecked the capital around 965, but scattered Khazars are still mentioned for another hundred years or more.

There is a deeper debate about what the Khazars really were. Some see them mainly as a Jewish kingdom, with the conversion at the center of the story. Others see a normal steppe empire that happened to gain a Jewish court, with trade and war mattering just as much. Both pictures use the same thin set of sources. They just choose to stress different parts of it.

general.thePeriod:
The Khazar khaganate occupied one of the great crossroads of the medieval world. Its heartland straddled the lower Volga basin and the steppe north of the Caucasus. This territory was a corridor, and along it the wealth of the northern forests flowed south toward the markets of the Islamic world. Furs, wax, honey, amber, and slaves descended the rivers, and Islamic silver returned in payment. The Khazars sat astride these routes and taxed everything that moved along them. That revenue sustained a state powerful enough to bargain as an equal with both Byzantium and the caliphate.

For roughly three centuries, from the middle of the seventh to the close of the tenth, the Khazars were the leading power of the western steppe. They emerged as the Western Turkic Khaganate broke apart, inheriting both its peoples and its claim to rule. What they built was no mere raiding confederation. It was a durable, partly settled empire, with fortified towns, a taxed economy, and a diplomacy that reached from Constantinople to Baghdad. To understand them, one must abandon the image of the steppe as empty wilderness, for the Khazars were, in effect, its managers.
general.theBoundaries:
The khaganate's frontiers were forged in a protracted struggle with the Arab caliphate. From the 640s into the late eighth century, Khazar and Arab armies clashed intermittently for control of the Caucasus passes, in a conflict known as the Arab–Khazar wars. The fighting swung both ways. Arab commanders sacked the Khazar capital more than once, while Khazar horsemen raided deep into what is now Azerbaijan and beyond. Around 737 a major Arab campaign forced a Khazar ruler to submit and, by some accounts, to accept Islam for a time, though the submission did not last. What endured instead was an uneasy stalemate along the mountains.

That stalemate mattered. Many historians argue that Khazar resistance blocked the caliphate from expanding north, sheltering the young societies of eastern Europe from Arab conquest, much as the Franks had checked the Arab advance in the west. The claim can be pushed too far, since the caliphate may have had little wish to hold the cold northern steppe in any case. But the Khazars were, at the very least, a formidable barrier on the caliphate's northern flank for a century.

The end came not from the south but from the north. In the tenth century the Rus' grew into a rival power along the rivers—warrior-merchants of Scandinavian descent who ruled over Slavic peoples. Around 965 the Kievan prince Sviatoslav stormed the Khazar fortress of Sarkel, and within a few years he destroyed the capital, Atil. The khaganate fell and did not revive.
general.theConditions:
Khazar power rested on the control of movement rather than the possession of land. The state straddled the Volga and Don river systems and the roads that linked them, drawing its money from tolls, from tribute paid by subject tribes, and from taxes on long-distance trade. Enormous hoards of Islamic silver coins, found along the Baltic and Russian river routes, show the scale of this commerce, much of which passed through Khazar hands. The empire also demanded furs and coin from the Slavic and Finnic peoples to its north and west—a dominance the Rus' would later inherit and turn against them.

The capital, Atil, showed the whole system in miniature. It stood where the Volga empties into the Caspian, a divided city of merchants, herders, and officials that was strikingly mixed in faith. Arab geographers describe a court with separate judges for Muslims, Christians, Jews, and pagans, an arrangement that let a multi-religious trading city settle its own disputes. The tolerance was practical. A state that lived on trade could not afford to alienate the merchants who carried it, and even-handedness kept the caravans and river boats moving.
general.internalDiversity:
Two features set the Khazar court apart. The first was its sacred dual kingship. Supreme authority nominally belonged to the khagan, a semi-divine figure hedged about by ritual and kept apart from daily rule, while real power lay with a deputy, the bek, who commanded the armies and set policy. Arab observers, used to rulers who governed as well as reigned, found the division remarkable and described it in detail.

The second feature, and the reason the Khazars are remembered at all, was the conversion of the ruling elite to Judaism. That some Khazar kings and nobles adopted Judaism is well attested across Arabic, Hebrew, and Byzantine sources, yet almost everything else about it is disputed. The traditional account, preserved in the letters known as the Khazar Correspondence, credits a ruler named Bulan with the choice and a later king, Obadiah, with strengthening Jewish practice. Scholars disagree sharply over the dating, with proposals scattered across the eighth and ninth centuries, and they disagree too over how deeply the change ran. The historian Constantine Zuckerman has argued for a fairly late date, near the middle of the ninth century. What the evidence does not support is a mass conversion of the whole people. The change seems to have been centered in the court and part of the nobility, while the wider population stayed mixed in faith.
general.longConsequences:
The khaganate's fall reshaped the region. With the Khazar toll-gate gone, the Rus' seized the river trade and the tribute networks the Khazars had built, and that inheritance helped consolidate the state centered on Kiev from which Russia and Ukraine trace their descent. The steppe frontier, no longer policed by a strong central power, passed to new nomadic groups such as the Pechenegs and later the Cumans. A world the Khazars had held together for three centuries came apart within a single generation.

The Khazars themselves largely vanish from the record after the eleventh century. Their language is lost, and because they left no writing of their own, their history must be built from outside sources and from archaeology. This silence has made them a screen onto which later ages project their concerns. The most damaging such projection is the modern claim that Ashkenazi Jews descend from Khazar converts rather than from ancient Judea, an idea the writer Arthur Koestler advanced in the 1970s and that fringe genetic work has revived since. Geneticists and historians have largely rejected it, finding instead that Ashkenazi populations share ancestry with other Jewish groups and with Middle Eastern populations. The theory has lasted less on the strength of its evidence than because it proves useful to those who want to deny Jewish ties to the Middle East.
general.periodizationDebate:
Dating the Khazar khaganate looks straightforward and proves anything but. A common span runs from the mid-seventh century, when the state broke free of the Western Turkic collapse, to Sviatoslav's campaigns around 965; yet each end blurs on closer scrutiny. The Khazars' separation from the Turkic world was gradual, and their political identity took decades to harden. At the other end, the sack of Atil did not wipe out every Khazar community at once, for scattered mentions of Khazars persist into the eleventh and twelfth centuries. The exact moment the state ceased to function cannot be fixed.

A deeper debate concerns how to describe the state at all. Older scholarship, shaped by Douglas Dunlop's mid-century history, treated the Judaism of the ruling house as the defining fact and the thread of the whole story. More recent work, including Peter Golden's studies of Khazar origins, folds the conversion into a broader picture in which the Khazars appear as a Turkic steppe empire whose religion is one feature among many, alongside trade, diplomacy, and tribute. The two framings are not truly opposed, yet they weigh the evidence differently, and they shape what a reader takes the Khazars to have been: a Jewish kingdom on the steppe, or a steppe empire that happened to have a Jewish court.

advanced.thePeriod:
No people so consequential to early medieval Eurasia is so nearly voiceless. The Khazars produced, so far as survives, not a single narrative history of themselves; their reconstruction rests on Arabic geographers, Byzantine officials, a handful of Hebrew documents of contested authenticity, and an archaeological record that is real but frustratingly mute on the questions that matter most. Every major claim about the Khazars is therefore a claim about outsiders' testimony, filtered through the purposes those outsiders had in writing. The result is a field unusually exposed to the interpreter's priorities, and unusually prone to being colonized by agendas foreign to the tenth century.

This evidentiary predicament frames the central analytical problem. The Khazars matter most for two things, a religious choice and a geopolitical function, and the sources bearing on each are precisely the sources whose reliability is most in question. To write Khazar history is to decide, repeatedly, how much weight a self-interested or much-later text can carry, knowing that no independent record exists to check it against.
advanced.theBoundaries:
The proposition that the Khazars shielded Europe from Islam has an appealing symmetry with the western legend of Poitiers, and it deserves the same skepticism. The Arab–Khazar wars were real and prolonged, and the caliphate's northern frontier did stabilize along the Caucasus. But the word shield imputes an intention and an outcome that the evidence underdetermines. It is far from clear the caliphate ever intended sustained conquest of the trans-Caucasian steppe, whose ecology suited neither its armies nor its fiscal base; the wars may reflect frontier raiding and prestige competition more than a thwarted drive for empire. The counterfactual of an Arab eastern Europe but for the Khazars cannot be tested, and it probably flatters its subject.

What can be defended is narrower and still significant. The Khazars fielded a state capable of absorbing and returning caliphal pressure for over a century, at a moment when few powers on the caliphate's periphery could. The campaign of the 730s that reportedly compelled a Khazar ruler's submission, sometimes described as a forced acceptance of Islam, shows both the caliphate's reach and the limits of its grip, since Khazar independence and the later turn to Judaism followed regardless. The interesting question is not whether the Khazars saved Europe but why a steppe polity proved so much more durable an adversary than the caliphate's other northern neighbors.
advanced.theConditions:
The Khazar economy is best understood as a protection-and-transit regime rather than a productive one. The state converted its control of chokepoints, the river confluences, the portages, and the Caucasus passes, into revenue, and it projected tributary demands over Slavic and Finnic peripheries whose furs and captives fed the Islamic silver trade. The distribution of silver coin hoards across the northern river routes is the hard evidence for a commercial system of surprising scale, though attributing any given hoard specifically to Khazar mediation requires inference the coins themselves do not supply. The model carried an inherent fragility that its later history exposed: a state whose wealth is transit revenue is only as strong as its monopoly over the routes, and when the Rus' broke that monopoly the fiscal foundation collapsed with it.

The sacred dual kingship raises a different order of question. The pattern of a ritually constrained, quasi-divine paramount alongside an executive deputy has analogues across Inner Asian and African polities, and comparative work has read it as a technique for stabilizing succession and diffusing the dangers of concentrated power. Whether the Khazar version actually functioned that way, or whether Arabic descriptions imposed a tidier structure than existed on the ground, remains genuinely uncertain. The sources are external, schematic, and written by observers with their own models of how a kingdom ought to look.
advanced.internalDiversity:
The conversion is where Khazar studies earns its difficulty. That a Khazar ruling stratum adopted Judaism is not seriously contested; the fact is corroborated across independent traditions with no obvious motive to invent it. The contested questions are three, and they compound. When did it occur, in the early eighth century as some read the traditional account, or the mid-ninth as Zuckerman argues from numismatic and missionary evidence? How complete was it, a personal royal cult, a court religion, or a genuine confessional transformation of an elite? And what did Judaism mean in a place with thin rabbinic infrastructure, normative rabbinic practice as the Hebrew sources claim in retrospect, or something more syncretic that later texts tidied into orthodoxy?

The stakes are not merely chronological. The Khazar Correspondence and the Cambridge Document, the fullest insider accounts, are exactly the sources whose authenticity and dating are least secure, and a scholar's reconstruction of the conversion tracks closely with how much credence those texts receive. Accept them broadly and the conversion becomes a datable, deliberate act of state, with named kings and a theological rationale; discount them and it recedes into a gradual, poorly documented drift knowable only in outline. There is no neutral vantage here, because there is no independent yardstick against which to test the disputed texts. This is the methodological knot at the center of the field, and it does not untie.
advanced.longConsequences:
The afterlife of the Khazars has been shaped less by what they did than by their convenient silence. Because they left no self-account and vanished as a polity, they became available for appropriation, and the modern Khazar hypothesis of Ashkenazi origins is the appropriation that matters. Its scholarly status is not genuinely in doubt: population-genetic studies place Ashkenazi Jews in a cluster sharing Middle Eastern and other Jewish ancestry, and the attempts to revive a Khazar origin, most prominently Eran Elhaik's, rest on contested methods and disputed proxy populations that specialists have largely declined to accept. The historical case is no stronger, for there is no evidentiary route from a converted steppe elite of at most a few thousand to the demographic mass of later Ashkenazi Jewry.

What merits analysis is not the hypothesis but its persistence. The theory has migrated from Koestler's well-meant 1976 popularization into antisemitic and anti-Zionist rhetoric, where its function is to sever Jews from any ancestral claim to the Middle East. This is a case study in how a marginal historical conjecture can acquire political utility wholly independent of its truth-value, and in how the empty spaces in a record, the very silence that makes the Khazars fascinating, become the spaces where ideology does its work.
advanced.periodizationDebate:
The hardest periodization question is not when the khaganate began or ended but what kind of object it was, because the answer determines what counts as its history. If the Khazars were essentially a Jewish polity, then the conversion is the pivot and everything bends toward it, as in the framing Dunlop's mid-century synthesis established. If they were essentially a Turkic steppe empire, one node in the long Inner Asian sequence of khaganates that Golden's philological work situates them within, then the conversion is an episode, and the deeper continuities run to the Türk and Bulgar states before them and the successor nomads after. The choice is not decidable on present evidence; it is a decision about which silence to privilege.

The consequences of that choice extend past the academy. A field whose central subject is nearly sourceless, and whose most famous fact has been weaponized in living political disputes, cannot treat its framing decisions as innocent. The responsible position holds two commitments at once: that the Judaism of the Khazar elite is a real and remarkable historical fact deserving precise study, and that this fact licenses none of the modern genealogical claims built upon it. Holding both, against pressure from opposite directions, is the discipline the Khazars specifically demand.

foundation.theStory: The Khazars pose a particular kind of historical problem: how to write the history of a people who left almost no words of their own. For three centuries a Turkic empire dominated the steppe between the Black and Caspian seas, taxed a continent's worth of trade, fought the Arab caliphate to a standstill, and saw its rulers adopt Judaism. Then it vanished, leaving its story to be told by neighbors, enemies, and much later commentators. Studying the Khazars means learning to build a picture from fragments, and being honest about where the picture goes blurry.
foundation.theDebate: Historians argue over three linked questions. When and how deeply did the Khazar elite convert to Judaism, in an early and thorough turn or a later and shallower one? Did the Khazars genuinely shield eastern Europe from Arab conquest, or is that a flattering overstatement of a frontier stalemate? And how should the state be characterized: as a Jewish kingdom that happened to sit on the steppe, or a steppe empire that happened to acquire a Jewish court? Each debate turns on how much trust to place in a small, contested body of sources.
foundation.whyItMatters: The Khazars open onto larger subjects: the workings of steppe empires and the river trade that linked northern Europe to the Islamic world, the diplomacy of small powers caught between giants, and the long conflict between the caliphate and its neighbors. They also offer a clear lesson in how gaps in the historical record get filled by later myth, most visibly the discredited claim that Ashkenazi Jews descend from Khazar converts, a useful case for thinking about how the past gets misused in the present.
questions: Why might a king caught between a Christian empire and a Muslim caliphate choose a third religion belonging to neither? What could such a choice gain him?
The Khazars left almost no writings of their own. How does that change what we can know about them, and how should a historian handle a story told only by outsiders?
A modern myth claims today's Ashkenazi Jews descend from the Khazars, though scholars reject it. Why do you think such an idea spread, and who might find it useful?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The modern field was largely founded by Douglas Dunlop, whose 1954 History of the Jewish Khazars made the conversion its center of gravity. Peter Golden's philological studies reframed the Khazars within the wider Turkic steppe world, treating their religion as one thread among many. On the conversion's date, Constantine Zuckerman has pressed for a mid-ninth-century timing against older readings that favored the eighth. Kevin Alan Brook has synthesized the archaeological and textual evidence for a broader audience. Running alongside is a separate and contentious literature on Ashkenazi genetics, in which mainstream population geneticists stand against Eran Elhaik's disputed revival of the Khazar-origin thesis.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence is thin and uneven. Arabic geographers such as Ibn Fadlan and al-Masudi supply valuable external description but wrote with their own aims. The Hebrew sources, the Khazar Correspondence, the Cambridge Document, and the Kievan Letter, would be decisive if secure, but their authenticity and dating are precisely what scholars dispute. Archaeology confirms a wealthy, plural, trading society and the reach of the silver economy, yet has yielded strikingly few objects marked as distinctly Jewish, which constrains claims about how far Judaism spread. The evidence can establish that a conversion happened and that the state was rich and strategically placed; it cannot settle the conversion's date, depth, or popular extent.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong argument about the Khazars must first declare how much weight it gives the disputed Hebrew sources, because nearly everything else follows from that. A defensible position runs roughly as follows: accept the conversion of the elite as securely attested across independent traditions; treat its precise dating and depth as unresolved and resist filling the gap with false precision; regard the shield-of-Europe claim as plausible in a limited, functional sense but unprovable as intention; and reject the Ashkenazi-origin hypothesis as unsupported by both the genetic and the historical evidence. The discipline lies in holding the remarkable fact of a Jewish steppe court apart from the modern claims later loaded onto it.
questions: How should a historian weigh the Khazar Correspondence, given that its authenticity is disputed and that accepting or discounting it largely determines one's account of the conversion?
Is the disagreement over whether the Khazars were a Jewish kingdom or a steppe empire with a Jewish court a factual dispute or a matter of framing? What would count as evidence for either view?

The Khazar-origin theory of Ashkenazi Jews is rejected by specialists yet persists in politics. What does its persistence reveal about the relationship between historical evidence and ideological usefulness?

research.topic: The conversion of the Khazar ruling elite to Judaism
research.content: That a Khazar ruling stratum adopted Judaism is a settled fact, corroborated across independent Arabic, Hebrew, and Byzantine traditions that had no shared motive to invent it. Contemporary Muslim geographers describe a Jewish king at Atil; the Hebrew Khazar Correspondence supplies an insider account; Byzantine sources register the anomaly of a Jewish steppe power. What is established is the elite conversion itself, not its details. The date, the depth, and the extent to which Judaism reached beyond the court remain open questions, and the consensus is careful to separate the secure core fact from the disputed particulars around it.

research.topic: Numismatic evidence for the date of the conversion
research.content: A line of research on coinage has sharpened the debate over when the conversion occurred. Khazar-minted coins imitating Islamic silver dirhams but carrying the Arabic legend 'Moses is the messenger of God,' dated to around 837 to 838 and recovered from hoards in the Baltic, have been read as evidence that the turn to Judaism was already underway by the 830s. This supports arguments, such as Constantine Zuckerman's, for a ninth-century rather than an eighth-century conversion. The coins demonstrate official use of a Jewish slogan rather than the depth of popular belief, however, and their interpretation is still contested among specialists.

research.topic: How far Judaism spread beyond the Khazar court
research.content: The share of the Khazar population that actually practiced Judaism cannot be recovered. What would resolve it is systematic settlement and burial archaeology showing distinctly Jewish practice across ordinary communities, together with internal records of religious life. That evidence is unavailable: few Khazar sites have been excavated, the capital Atil has never been securely located and likely lies beneath the shifting Volga delta, distinctly Jewish objects are almost absent from the finds, and the Khazars kept no surviving records of their own. What can be responsibly inferred is that the conversion was concentrated in the court and part of the nobility while the wider population stayed religiously mixed. What must remain unknown is the true proportion of Khazars who were Jewish and whether their practice was rabbinic or more syncretic.

comparativeNarrative.name: A Kingdom of One's Own
comparativeNarrative.content: For medieval Jews living under Christian and Muslim rule, a Jewish king on the steppe was astonishing. Hasdai ibn Shaprut, a Jewish official in Muslim Spain, wrote to the Khazar king Joseph. He hoped to confirm that a free Jewish realm truly existed. In later Jewish memory Khazaria became proof that Jewish political power had once been real. This view captures something genuine. It holds the emotional weight of a Jewish state for a people who had none. Its risk is inflation. It can swell a court religion into a full Jewish nation. It can also read modern longings back into a murky and thinly recorded medieval fact.

comparativeNarrative.name: One Khaganate Among Many
comparativeNarrative.content: Seen from the history of the steppe, the Khazars belong to a long line of Turkic empires. The Türk and Bulgar states came before them. The Pechenegs and Cumans came after. All of them shared sacred kingship, tribute economies, and mobile power. This view refuses to treat the conversion as the whole story. It sees a familiar kind of steppe state that happened to adopt an unusual faith. That recovers the Khazars' ordinary strategic logic and their place in a wider world. Its danger is the reverse of the first view. By making the Khazars typical, it can play down how singular the choice of Judaism really was among rulers who more often took Islam or Christianity.

comparativeNarrative.name: The View From the Capitals
comparativeNarrative.content: Nearly everything written about the Khazars comes from those who dealt with them. Arab geographers catalogued the north. Byzantine officials weighed an ally against the caliphate. Rus chroniclers recorded a rival's fall. Each described the Khazars for its own ends. This outside view is vital, since it is almost all we have, and it captures the Khazars' place in a wide web of trade and war. But it is a hall of mirrors. We meet the Khazars only through the interests of people who taxed them, allied with them, or destroyed them. We never hear them in a voice that is clearly their own.

comparativeSummary: Set side by side, these vantages reveal a people defined largely by what others needed them to be. To medieval and later Jews, Khazaria was proof that Jewish sovereignty had once existed; to historians of the steppe, it was one Turkic empire in a long line; to the states around it, it was a partner, a rival, or a threat to be recorded and used. None of these is simply wrong, and their disagreement is the point: because the Khazars left no account of themselves, every portrait of them is also a portrait of the observer. The underlying argument is whether the conversion sits at the center of Khazar history or counts as one striking feature of an otherwise ordinary steppe state, and how much of the story any of the thin sources can really bear.

rabbitHole.label: The Byzantine Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Christian empire on the Khazars' western flank was their most important ally for much of the period, cooperating against the caliphate and binding the two dynasties through imperial marriage. Byzantine records are also a main source for Khazar history.

rabbitHole.label: The Umayyad Caliphate
rabbitHole.reason: The Arab–Khazar wars against the Umayyads defined the khaganate's southern frontier and underlie the claim that the Khazars blocked Arab expansion into eastern Europe. Understanding that conflict is prerequisite to understanding the Khazar state.

rabbitHole.label: The Vikings
rabbitHole.reason: The Rus', warrior-merchants of Scandinavian origin, grew into the rival river power whose prince Sviatoslav destroyed Sarkel and Atil around 965 and inherited the trade and tribute networks the Khazars had built.

rabbitHole.label: The Steppe
rabbitHole.reason: The Khazar khaganate was a product of the Eurasian grassland world, with its mounted nomads, tributary economies, and sacred kingship. The steppe supplies the environmental and political grammar of everything the Khazars became.

rabbitHole.label: The Silk Road
rabbitHole.reason: The Khazars taxed the northern arm of Eurasian commerce, the Volga and Caspian routes that carried furs and slaves south and Islamic silver north. Their wealth is a case study in how control of trade corridors builds a state.

rabbitHole.label: The Jewish Diaspora
rabbitHole.reason: The discredited Khazar hypothesis wrongly folds Ashkenazi Jews into a supposed Khazar rather than Middle Eastern descent, making the Khazars a recurring flashpoint in arguments about Jewish origins and belonging.

rabbitHole.label: The Abbasid Caliphate
rabbitHole.reason: The caliphate that succeeded the Umayyads remained the Khazars' great southern neighbor, the source of the silver that flowed through their markets and of the geographers who left much of the surviving description of them.

reference.author: Dunlop, Douglas M.
reference.title: The History of the Jewish Khazars
reference.annotation: The founding modern synthesis of Khazar history, which made the conversion its organizing thread and assembled the Arabic and Hebrew evidence into a single narrative. Later work revises its emphases, but it remains the starting point the field argues with.

reference.author: Golden, Peter B.
reference.title: Khazar Studies: An Historico-Philological Inquiry into the Origins of the Khazars
reference.annotation: The philological foundation for Khazar origins, language, and names, which situates the khaganate within the Turkic steppe sequence rather than defining it solely by the conversion. The counterweight to conversion-centered readings.

reference.author: Brook, Kevin Alan
reference.title: The Jews of Khazaria
reference.annotation: The fullest recent single-volume synthesis of the textual and archaeological evidence, written to be usable by non-specialists while tracking the live scholarly disputes over dating, depth, and the genetic question.

reference.author: Golden, Peter B.; Ben-Shammai, Haggai; Róna-Tas, András (eds.)
reference.title: The World of the Khazars: New Perspectives
reference.annotation: The standard reference collection, gathering specialist essays that map the current state of debate on chronology, the conversion, and the reliability of the sources. Where the field's disagreements are laid out in detail.

reference.author: Golb, Norman; Pritsak, Omeljan
reference.title: Khazarian Hebrew Documents of the Tenth Century
reference.annotation: Edits and translates the key Hebrew documents, including the Kievan Letter, on which the insider evidence and the authenticity debates rest. Lets a reader confront the disputed primary texts directly.

reference.author: Zuckerman, Constantine
reference.title: On the Date of the Khazars' Conversion to Judaism and the Chronology of the Kings of the Rus Oleg and Igor
reference.annotation: The influential case for a mid-ninth-century conversion date, argued from numismatic and missionary evidence. The article the entry leans on when it describes the dating dispute as genuinely open.

commerce.title: The Jews of Khazaria
commerce.author: Brook, Kevin Alan
commerce.note: The most approachable full account. Brook walks a general reader through the evidence and the arguments without assuming any background, and it is the natural place to begin.

commerce.title: The History of the Jewish Khazars
commerce.author: Dunlop, Douglas M.
commerce.note: The classic 1954 narrative that founded the modern field. Drier and older than newer work, but still the book serious readers cite; turn to it to see where the questions came from.

commerce.title: The World of the Khazars: New Perspectives
commerce.author: Golden, Peter B.; Ben-Shammai, Haggai; Róna-Tas, András (eds.)
commerce.note: A specialist essay collection for readers ready to follow the disputes in detail. This is where the current arguments over dating and sources actually take place.

commerce.title: Khazaria in the Ninth and Tenth Centuries
commerce.author: Zhivkov, Boris
commerce.note: A dense modern monograph reconstructing the khaganate's politics and economy from the scattered evidence. Reach for it once the introductions no longer satisfy and a summary will not do.

commerce.title: Khazarian Hebrew Documents of the Tenth Century
commerce.author: Golb, Norman; Pritsak, Omeljan
commerce.note: For the primary Hebrew texts themselves, edited and translated. The place to weigh the disputed insider sources with your own eyes instead of taking a historian's word for them.

popularCulture.title: The Kuzari (c. 1140, Judah Halevi)
popularCulture.description: Around 1140 the Jewish poet Judah Halevi wrote The Kuzari, a book of ideas built around the Khazar king's choice of faith. In it the king questions a philosopher, a Christian, a Muslim, and finally a rabbi, then picks Judaism. Halevi used the Khazar story as a frame for his own beliefs. The real conversion barely shaped the book; the famous debate is mostly his invention.

popularCulture.title: Dictionary of the Khazars (1984, Milorad Pavić)
popularCulture.description: This puzzle-like novel imagines the Khazar conversion as a lost mystery, told through three made-up encyclopedias, one Christian, one Muslim, one Jewish, that all disagree about what happened. It even came in male and female editions with a single different page. The book turns the real gap in the record into playful fiction. Enjoy it as invention, because almost none of it is history.

popularCulture.title: The Wind of the Khazars (2001, Marek Halter)
popularCulture.description: This adventure novel ties a modern journalist to the medieval Khazar court to bring the lost Jewish kingdom to life for general readers. It is vivid and fast-moving. Like most historical fiction, it fills the many blanks in the record with confident invention, so its scenes reach far beyond what anyone can actually know about the Khazars.

popularCulture.title: The Thirteenth Tribe (1976, Arthur Koestler)
popularCulture.description: In this popular book the writer Arthur Koestler argued that most Jews descend from the Khazars rather than from ancient Israel. It spread the idea to a wide audience. Historians and geneticists have since found little to back it, and antisemites have used the claim to attack Jewish ties to the Middle East. It is remembered now as an influential mistake.

popularCulture.title: The 'Khazar myth' in modern politics
popularCulture.description: The idea that today's Jews are really Khazars keeps resurfacing online and in extremist talk. People use it to claim that Jews have no true link to the land of Israel. Scholars reject the theory, yet it survives because it is useful to those who want to deny that link. It is a clear example of history being bent to fit a cause.
